article_title: California Awards $3.5 Billion Contract to Lay the Nation's First True High-Speed Rail Track
summary: Submission approvable with 1 minor warning(s) — file a corrections record on merge

content_preview.title: California Awards $3.5 Billion Contract to Lay the Nation's First True High-Speed Rail Track
content_preview.summary: A Kiewit-led consortium will electrify 119 miles of Central Valley guideway for trains running up to 220 mph, with tracklaying set to begin before year-end.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The California High-Speed Rail Authority's board on June 1, 2026 approved the launch of the initial phase of track and systems installation, clearing the way for crews to begin laying what the Authority calls the nation's first true high-speed rail track, according to the [California High-Speed Rail Authority](https://hsr.ca.gov/2026/06/01/news-release-california-approves-team-to-build-nations-first-true-high-speed-rail-track-and-systems/). A consortium of Kiewit, Stacy Witbeck, and...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (580 words, within the 400-1200 News range). Clear Overview / What We Know / Funding and Political Context / What We Don't Know sections. Every paragraph is attributed inline to a named source. Neutral, restrained framing appropriate for a developing infrastructure story.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots read from disk. source-1.html.gz (Metro Magazine) and source-2.html.gz (Railway News) and source-3.html.gz (IndexBox) captured full article content and corroborate the article verbatim. source-0.html.gz (hsr.ca.gov) returned a 212-byte Incapsula bot-block page (HTTP 200) and source-4.html.gz (PBS) returned an AWS WAF JavaScript-challenge page (HTTP 202) — neither snapshot contains the cited content. CRITICALLY, every claim attributed to those two blocked sources is independently verified elsewhere: the Authority's headline/lead claims, the Ian Choudri CEO quote, the 119-mile / 220-mph / Nov-2025-procurement / 60-completed-structures+30-underway / 171-miles-Merced-to-Bakersfield / nearly-19,200-jobs / up-to-1,700-workers-daily figures all appear verbatim in the full-content Metro Magazine snapshot (which carries the Authority's own release text). The PBS-attributed Trump quote, Authority spokesperson quote, $4B July-2025 federal withdrawal, dropped lawsuit, and 'more than $100 billion' total cost were verified via a last-resort live WebFetch of the PBS URL (recorded here because the committed snapshot was WAF-blocked); all five matched verbatim.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: No hallucinations detected. Choudri quote verbatim and correctly attributed to the Authority/CEO (confirmed in Metro). Trump quote 'The Railroad we were promised still does not exist, and never will' verbatim and correctly attributed (PBS live). Authority spokesperson quote verbatim and correctly attributed (PBS live). The conflicting top-speed figure across sources (Railway News '354 km/h' vs IndexBox '355 km/h') was correctly AVOIDED — the article uses only '220 mph' (2 occurrences, no km/h figure). The disputed $126B/$230B cost figures (which trace to a bot-blocked Fox source) were correctly DROPPED in favor of PBS's 'more than $100 billion'. No headline or lead claim rests on a bot-blocked snapshot — Metro and live PBS independently cover them all.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism, no editorializing, no AI self-reference. Political context is presented even-handedly (Trump's criticism and the Authority's rebuttal both quoted).
editor_notes.originality: Genuinely new topic. No prior high-speed rail coverage exists in the archive (grep across src/content/articles/ returned nothing). Not a duplicate.
concerns: Two of five source snapshots (hsr.ca.gov via Incapsula, PBS via AWS WAF) captured bot-block/challenge pages rather than article content despite HTTP 200/202 status codes. The provenance snapshot chain for those two URLs therefore does not contain the cited text. This is the basis for the corrections record; the underlying claims are nonetheless independently verified (Metro snapshot for the Authority content, live WebFetch for PBS).

Four of five domains (hsr.ca.gov, metro-magazine.com, railwaynews.net, indexbox.io) are not in config/source_allowlist.txt. All are reputable for this story — hsr.ca.gov is the official primary government source, Metro Magazine and Railway News are established rail trade publications, and IndexBox is a market-intelligence outlet — so this is a soft warning, not a factual defect.
recommendations: Consider adding hsr.ca.gov (official primary government source), metro-magazine.com, and railwaynews.net to config/source_allowlist.txt for future rail/infrastructure coverage.
Future submissions citing hsr.ca.gov or pbs.org should anticipate bot-block/WAF snapshot failures and lean on full-content corroborating sources.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Factually clean, neutral, original News piece. The only issue is provenance hygiene: two source snapshots captured bot-block pages, which a public corrections note honestly documents. Every factual claim — headline, lead, quotes, and specifics — is independently verified against full-content snapshots and a last-resort live PBS fetch. APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS: publish with a corrections record documenting the two failed snapshots and confirming the claims were independently verified.
